2606.25108.title: The Clinician's Veto: Navigating Trust, Liability, and Uncertainty in Autonomous AI Prescribing
2606.25108.paper_summary: Autonomous AI systems are transitioning from advisory to autonomous roles for medication prescriptions. Recent United States bill H.R. 238 and Utah's prescription-renewal pilot both authorize AI to prescribe medications in an agentic capacity. While some regulatory guidelines suggest aggregate model performance metrics for clearance, they do not require i) calibrated per-prediction confidence for action-gated thresholds, ii) differentiated communication of uncertainty arising from model ignorance (epistemic) versus genuine clinical ambiguity (aleatoric), and iii) inferential transparency at the moment of decision that allows for liability allocation. Here, we present a regulatory and technical argument (tested with a survey of 136 U.S. prescribing clinicians) positioning these as minimum architectural requirements for safe autonomous prescribing. Our results suggest prescribing clinicians i) would not permit autonomous prescribing without a calibrated confidence-based escalation mechanism, ii) preferred a competing-options summary when uncertainty was aleatoric but shifted to abstention when uncertainty was epistemic, and iii) were only willing to accept additional liability when inferential transparency enabled a substantive judgment under acknowledged uncertainty. These findings indicate our recommended architectural features would encourage higher rates of clinician adoption, largely through collapsing much of what "autonomy" conventionally means. A system meeting these requirements would function less as an autonomous agent and more as a heavily supervised decision-support tool. As legislation and state pilots proceed, our technical argument backed by clinician perspectives provides opportunities for regulation to constrain the degree of autonomy ethically granted to AI in prescribing while aligning liability with the institutional actors who control system design and deployment.
2606.25108.paper_summary_zh: 自主 AI 系統正在從建議角色轉變為自主藥物處方角色。最近的美國法案 H.R. 238 和猶他州的處方續期試點都授權 AI 以代理身份開立藥物。雖然一些監管指導方針建議使用總體模型性能指標來進行批准，但它們並不要求 i) 針對行動門檻的每次預測進行校準的信心，ii) 區分由模型無知（認識論的）引起的不確定性與真正臨床模糊性（隨機的）之間的溝通，以及 iii) 在決策時的推理透明度，以便進行責任分配。在這裡，我們提出了一個監管和技術論點（通過對 136 名美國處方臨床醫師的調查進行測試），將這些視為安全自主處方的最低架構要求。我們的結果表明，處方臨床醫師 i) 不會允許在沒有基於校準信心的升級機制的情況下進行自主處方，ii) 在不確定性為隨機時偏好競爭選項摘要，但在不確定性為認識論時則轉向放棄，iii) 只有在推理透明度使得在承認不確定性的情況下能夠進行實質判斷時，才願意接受額外的責任。這些發現表明，我們推薦的架構特徵將促進臨床醫師的更高採納率，主要是通過縮減「自主性」的傳統意義。滿足這些要求的系統將更像是一個受到嚴格監督的決策支持工具，而不是一個自主代理。隨著立法和州試點的推進，我們基於臨床醫師觀點的技術論點為監管提供了機會，以倫理地限制授予 AI 在處方中的自主性程度，同時將責任與控制系統設計和部署的機構行為者對齊。
2606.25108.author: Eileanor LaRocco et.al.
2606.25108.authors: Eileanor LaRocco, Sarah Tan, Adarsh Subbaswamy, Anne Andrews, Andrew Taylor, Cree Gaskin, Chirag Agarwal
